body:
> simple dialogs like prompt, confirm, or alert are usually called in the same way that the corresponding built in browser functionality is, through an imperative API

Simple is the key point here, I think.

Dialogs can range from simple to really complex, while toasts are usually always simple with a basic render structure, where the hook to pass the data payload works well

It feels like this issue is asking about support for the equivalent of https://mui.com/toolpad/core/react-use-dialogs/, that we build to try to fix: https://github.com/mui/material-ui/issues/24759.

This seems to have a large product surface area: https://github.com/sweetalert2/sweetalert2/issues?q=is%3Aissue%20state%3Aclosed, 1.6k closed issues 😅.

@atomiks there is a strong point in favor of custom dialogs as well however, resetting state

with the declarative approach, the only that baseUI currently supports for dialogs, the user is in charge of resetting state when a dialog is closed

if one could call `dialogManager.custom(<MyDialog />)`, baseUI could render the dialog with a different key every time.

is the difference between simple toasts or dialogs like prompt/confirm/alert and custom content any more than simply allowing the user to pass a react component?

I implemented this functionality for myself all in one `Dialog.tsx` file, sharing it here for reference:

https://gist.github.com/marcospgp/9ae67de36dcc212a76ec1755b3c97c72

@atomiks The main reason for my implementation of `dialogManager` was to solve the issue of resetting dialog state

If you don't provide a solution to this problem, which to me seems clearly fundamental to the nature of dialogs, then users will be likely to each implement their own approach with varying levels of success.

There may be tradeoffs between different implementations, but leaving the state reset issue as an unsolved problem to me seems like the worst of the options.

If you can think of a more elegant solution, I would be happy to hear about it and would be able to change my implementation accordingly. But for now, this is the best I could think of.
@marcospgp do you have an example of the state reset problem? I would expect that closing the dialog unmounts it's children, so clear the state.

@oliviertassinari if you have a dialog like the example one shown in the documentation: ```jsx import * as React from 'react'; import { Dialog } from '@base-ui-components/react/dialog'; export default function ExampleDialog() { return ( View notifications Notifications You are all caught up. Good job!
Close
); } ``` and you add some state to it, say `const [state, setState] = useState("something")` then closing the dialog will not reset this state. you need to define the dialog as a standalone component, say ``, then wherever you want to use it, you have to add some logic to update its `key` prop to a new value upon closing, to force its state to be reset. you can also add some logic to the open state handler to manually reset each state variable, but this is fragile as it is easy to forget to update it when the dialog state changes (as in a new variable is introduced). updating the `key` prop is failure proof clearly it doesn't make much sense to repeat this logic everywhere you want to use a dialog

@marcospgp oh, I see, thanks, it's super clear now.

Personal opinion on this:
- It seems the same as with any dialog component. For example with Material UI, there, I don't recall seeing that much complains about it, people would put `<Dialog.Portal>` inside it's own component, e.g. https://mui.com/material-ui/react-dialog/.
- Maybe it's more a problem here because: the docs doesn't encourage to create a component for the dialog portal.
- dialog manager could maybe help with state reset, but it feels more about convinience for standard dialogs cases.

> I implemented this functionality for myself all in one Dialog.tsx file, sharing it here for reference

Could you also share an example of how to use it? I think it could help the next engineer landing on this issue. Thanks.

I think the main reason to provide a dialog manager is that it is far from trivial for users to implement a solution on their own.

There are many pitfalls to be aware of, such as resetting dialog state by updating the `key` prop on dialog open or close, or if rendering a dialog programmatically (such as in an event handler, for example if an exception occurs), doing so with open set to false so that it will play CSS transitions appropriately when open is set to true in a *separate* render cycle, and possibly others I'm not aware of.

Sorry guys, I don't mean to keep spamming this issue but I kept iterating on the dialog manager so I wanted to share its current state.

Here's the gist: https://gist.github.com/marcospgp/330c459dca8790955e19f1384f396260

I don't want to make this more verbose with a long description. I think you can understand what's going on by asking an assistant like Claude about it. Cheers!

419 -> 311 lines, so a big win in simplification

In https://github.com/mui/base-ui/pull/2974 I implemented an alternative way of controlling dialogs: you can set up "handles" for common dialogs and trigger them throughout your application with Dialog.Trigger parts without having to place them in Dialog.Root. Triggers also support custom payload that can affect what gets rendered in the Dialog.

Does this solve the problems you described here?

This feature will be available in the next npm release of Base UI.

In the meantime, you can try it out on our Canary release channel:

```sh
npm i https://pkg.pr.new/@base-ui-components/react@2974
```

performed_via_github_app.name: GitHub Actions
performed_via_github_app.description: Automate your workflow from idea to production

body:
for those arriving from search engines: stale scope capture caused by imperative APIs in react can be mitigated by passing a component reference instead of an instance:

```
dialogManager.custom(MyDialog)
```

instead of

```
dialogManager.custom(<MyDialog prop={thisValueIsNowStale} />)
```

this forces you to write a dedicated component for each dialog and makes it hard to capture stale scope.

the tradeoff is that it's also not possible to customize the component you're passing. if you want to do so, you have to update state externally (such as in a global store with something like zustand, or in localstorage, etc.) and have the dedicated component read from there.

or a bit more hacky, define the component inside the component that will be spawning it, in a subfunction (but this captures stale scope).

this comment mostly in response to this: https://github.com/mui/base-ui/issues/2802#issuecomment-3318998619

@marcospgp thanks for the thoughtful discussion + gist

You may be interested in how a classic package `react-modal-hook` tackled similar issue with stale data. 
It used to be somewhat common and still has 40K+ weekly on npm. It doesn't appear to be maintained any more.

In short its `useModal()` hook takes an optional deps array.

While some devs may argue and balk at that idea deps arrays are frankly a core concept of react and using hooks so it actually does feel a bit idiomatic.

See the answer to this issue where there's an example: https://github.com/mpontus/react-modal-hook/issues/31

I think the library would have gotten way more popular too if it shipped with a handful more examples that showed these features.

This is a really interesting discussion.

The part that resonates most with me is not only the imperative vs declarative API debate, but the fact that dialogs often sit in a gray area between UI primitive and application workflow.

For simple alert/confirm/prompt cases, an imperative API feels very natural. But once the dialog owns local state, async actions, validation, nested flows, or custom content, the real problem becomes lifecycle ownership: who is responsible for mounting, resetting, closing, transitions, and avoiding stale closures?

In my experience, the safest pattern is usually not “make every dialog imperative”, but to separate two cases clearly:

1. simple system dialogs: alert, confirm, prompt
2. application dialogs: dedicated components with explicit lifecycle boundaries

A manager can be very useful, but only if it makes that boundary obvious. Otherwise it risks becoming a second rendering model hidden outside the normal React tree.

The state reset problem mentioned above is real though. Even when the final API is not a full dialog manager, I think the docs should strongly guide users toward extracting dialog content into a dedicated component when local state is involved.
